speaker: interviewer
text: Richard's just fixing my microphone [BACKROUND NOISE] because there was something wrong. So you want to just--

speaker: interviewee
text: So I have-- my story really only one hour and fifteen minutes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Check, check.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: Channel 2-- channel 2.

speaker: interviewer
text: You see, there's an argument that says that the tape is about you. And you shouldn't get into what happened so much to us--

speaker: interviewee
text: They're wrong.

speaker: interviewer
text: But, but what--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'll tell you when. And you just roll, name, and straight into it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape 2, Esther Bem. Tell me what happened when you left that room in the fall of 1941.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father found a connection-- he paid with tremendous amount of money-- who led us from Zagreb on a-- on the train. It was an Ustasha officer. And they were war profiteers. Some of them didn't care. Or, [LAUGHS] I mean, they were not so political. Or it was not the ideology. It was just money. So they made money on, on these people that were running away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were you with?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the officer of the Ustasha-- my mother, my father, and I. First, we started by train. And then we arrived to a village where we slept overnight in some barn. Everything was beautifully organized. Then we walked a whole night, climbing up and down. Then we reached a river, and we were taken by a little riverboat. Then we arrived to another village and slept over. This went days and days. And we finally arrived to Italian zone in Ljubljana.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what you took with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember we had [SIGHS] good shoes. I had good shoes that kept me going for a while [LAUGHS] until I didn't grow out of them. We had a few little pieces of summer clothes, some winter clothes. We had-- I think we had some cushions with us. I don't remember. But the one thing I know-- this was something that my parents looked after-- I was carrying with them, but I don't know exactly what. But I only know that at, at night, in one of the homes that we stayed in a barn, they, they stole whatever they could from our knapsacks. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we arrived to Ljubljana, which was Italian zone, which was a sanctuary finally. We found a room. It was not difficult. There was a lot of Jews living in Ljubljana. And my father went everyday to the coffeehouse and came home with all kinds of information.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we registered at the Italian registry municipality. They were very kind to us. They said, well, you are refugees now. We'll look after you. Wait. We will let you know when you are going into inner of Italy. You cannot stay in Ljubljana. There were too many refugees. This was just the place to collect us. And we stayed there a-- I don't know, a few months, maybe two months. And we got the notification-- or I think my father had to go regularly to register to see what's happening. And they told him, yes, you and your family will take a train. And you will be assigned to a place called Provincia Treviso in a village called Possagno. And you will be 'internati civili di guerra,' which means you will become civil prisoners of war. And we went. And we arrived to this village. There were about 10 families there already from Poland, from Belgrade, from Sarajevo, from Vienna-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think that's about all. We were seven children between the ages of 7 and 13. We were four girls and three boys. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have to say that these were two quite good years. We didn't go to school. We had very little money. But there was some kind of help, which was a Jewish agency-- Italian-Jewish agency like you have here, United Appeal. They had this in Italy, that they were helping us. How this came to us-- I think through the municipality of that village. Very minimal-- we had, really, the minimal to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your room like?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived with some Italian woman whose husband was in the war. We had a room. And we stayed all day in the kitchen with her because it was getting cold, and they didn't heat the rooms. But she was very kind to us. And we were-- we were clever children, I think. And we were trying to learn from each other what we could. We had no Italian friends. We were not allowed-- at least the first year. But then we made friends. And we were standing and waiting in front of the school when they came out of school. [LAUGHS] We stood there, and then we played with them. And we had a good time. We were reading a lot at random, whatever we could put our hands on. Our parents were trying to teach us. They were trying to discipline us. But-- they tried, but it didn't work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So whatever we learned was on our own. We, we learned Italian very, very thoroughly, the grammar and from books. We tried a bit of English to learn from each other. We read a lot. We painted. We occupied ourselves. Tell me about a typical day. You got up in the morning--

speaker: interviewee
text: Got up in the morning. We were a lot hungry because the food-- we had the ration cards. And we had that much bread and that much oil, that much-- what we could. We had no money for extras. My mother was cautious. So there was meat-- out of question. The others had pork. We didn't, which was not good. But that's what she decided. We, we walked a lot, very nature. We were very, very close, the seven of us. We were like one. We never had fights. I don't even remember that. And the-- one of the women-- girls in that group was my best friend. And we could share everything and tell each other everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was two very nice years in comparison what other people had, except we were hungry. We had no clothes. And we, we were cold. But it was not fear and not desperation.

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew the war is still going on strong. We knew there is no end in sight. And every night, we were-- one of the parents would go and listen to Radio London in an attic. So then he would come back and tell, in one of the kitchens, what was going on the front.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did all seven children live in the same house or in different houses?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, different houses but close by. It was a tiny village. Now, in 1943, the Germans occupied Italy. And then everything changed. In the middle of the night of the night-- in-- September 8, when the Germans occupied Italy, of 1943-- an unknown person arrived to our door, knocked at night, and said, run. Disappear. And don't go together. Each one his own way find. Don't ask your neighbors where they are going. I don't want you to go together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I will now tell you something about it. Many, many, many years later, I found out what happened through somebody who was one of the children-- became very active in Jerusalem at the university with the Yad Vashem. And he was sent by Yad Vashem to that village to find out what happened. Why did this man come and notify everybody, disappear?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened was the following. There was a meeting in the municipality with the fascists and the Germans. The Germans occupied now every village. And they, at this meeting, in the protocol said, tomorrow morning, at 6:00, you pick up all those Jews. Put them-- the train is waiting. Put them on the train to Rome. And from Rome, you know where they are going-- to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the meeting was over, very late at night, this fascist went from house to house to notify us. And, and in the morning, they came with the police officially to pick us up. And we were gone. What percentage of the Jews in the village were gone?

speaker: interviewee
text: All of us-- there were no other Jews except us refugees. We were refugees. Now we really listened to him. He was very stern about it. Don't ask anybody where they are. Everybody where he can find his way out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Some of them were a little, little slow. There was this family with these two boys who were really slow to pack. What do we take? What don't we take? They couldn't leave. It was too late.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were stuck two years in that village almost bricked in a house that looked like an abandoned house at the-- like, at the outskirts of the village. And they were fed-- every night somebody brought them food. And that's how they spent the-- this is the boy that later on became obsessed with the Holocaust and studied it at university in Jerusalem and became part of Yad Vashem team.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Iko Garti. I only once saw him when I came to Israel and never again. He was very bizarre-- became very bizarre. [LAUGHS] Anyway, my parents and I took what we could, very little. My father was always very alert when it came to disappearing and running. He-- this was our luck. He never was concerned what are we taking, what are we not taking. He just wanted to leave because we were told. And he knew this is it. And we left. We started climbing mountains. There were mountains behind that village. It was a very picturesque, beautiful village. But we never went to-- so high into those mountains. We would go at the outskirts of the mountain. And we climbed. And we climbed. And we saw huts abandoned, like-- abandoned. But people lived there. And we knocked at somebody's door in the middle of the night. And I remember distinctly-- this was a family with three sons. And their, their names was Primo, Secondo, and Terzo-- First, Second, and Third. That's what-- their names. We said-- we were desperate. I mean, in desperation, you, you are not trying to convince them yes or no. We told them, look, we are refugees. We are Jews. We have to hide. The Germans are after us. Take us in. They did. They took us in. They found-- cleaned up a room, gave us a bed, and kept us a few days. But they were too close to the road. And they said to us, after a few days, very kindly, they would like to help us. But they can't because they are so close to the road that many people are coming and going. But they found neighbors who are really in the depth of the mountain. They will take us in.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No idea. It was a father, a widower with two grown-up children, a daughter and a son, who worked on the fields. Kind, poor-- they were at the bottom of the economic scale. You can imagine. They were sharecroppers. They didn't have their own land. They worked for some landlord in Italy, in maybe Milan or, or Rome. And they shared the crop. Like, whatever was sold, half of it belonged to them. So they had very-- almost no money, just to eat, and I bet something was probably for them to buy a, a new dress for the daughter. And they were terribly poor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they able to feed you and your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: They fed us. They kept us, never complained, never looked for excuses. They were warm to us. We never felt that we are intruding. This was the big thing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was a real lesson in human decency.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were with them a few months. [SIGHS] And then temporarily, they told-- what was beautiful about this family was they were very good singers. And they sang for us every night operas.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after a few months, they got scared because some neighbors started questioning, who are these strangers you are keeping? Oh, well, they just came. They are here overnight. They will go. They will come.

speaker: interviewee
text: They got very scared. And we knew that we cannot stay anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you afraid?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fear was a constant emotion that we lived. I mean, fear in the belly was something we lived all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the first time that you were afraid?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll tell you about it. I was afraid all the time because we never knew what comes tomorrow. Somebody may walk into their house who can denounce us, just go somewhere and tell they have these foreigners here. And they would come and take us. I mean, they knew that our destiny is a very, very fragile.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have identification papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had. But we couldn't use them because we were Jewish. We were Jews from Yugoslavia. Imagine this to show. I mean, this was Auschwitz written on the wall for us right away. And I'll tell you, in 1943, when the Germans occupied Italy, Eichmann-- who was the head of the Gestapo for extermination of Jews-- he sent very well-trained, efficient Jew hunters to Italy. But they were not successful. And I'll tell you why-- because the population did not cooperate. They were not ready to cooperate in the Final Solution of Jews. They were not ready to accept the model of destroying Jews the way the Germans did.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they would rather play the role of inefficient people, people that are not always together, that are forgetful. When they would get in a document, now, go to Rome and pick up those Jews, they would say, we didn't find them. And that's how the Italian people behaved.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now these people where we lived, they were religious Catholics. They were the most altruistic people that you can find in the world. Imagine that we totally changed their everyday life. This is not somebody who came just and gave donation to save those Jews. They had to change every minute of their life. They did not have water-- running water, no electricity. They had to carry water from some high well on the top of the mountain.

speaker: interviewer
text: So here we are three more people to carry for them the, the water. And we couldn't help them because we were not to be seen outdoors. Could you ever go out of their house?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did-- outdoor a little bit. But we, [SIGHS] we didn't dare. If somebody walks by, there were neighbors. There were people. They, they to church on Sunday. So they knew-- they had friends. But they eliminated their social life totally. I mean, what these people did for me, I, I wouldn't like to be through-- put to such a test. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I remember, recall, that they found another home for us for a very short time. I have very little recollection of this home. We stayed there a few weeks. And then they couldn't keep us. They were afraid. They found another-- they never let us go-- 'just go and find a place.' They found another home for us. So here we came now. Here we now are on top of the mountain-- a big family, many brothers, many wives. I mean wives-- three, four women; three, four men; an old mother; a lot of children. Poor-- I can't tell you how poor they were. And this was already end of the winter, like, February.

speaker: interviewee
text: '44. We stayed with them. They were kind. They shared with us their premises. They shared with us their food, everything. We felt so welcome. We never felt that we are intruding, and they are now coming up with some excuses that we should go. We had nowhere to go anymore. We, really-- there was nowhere to go. And one day, we were in the barn. Most of the time we were in the barn because it was warm. One of the sons came in in a panic, running. He says, go, go, run. The Germans are here. And there were rumors that the Germans want to build an observation point at the top of that mountain to see the comings and goings of the partisans and, of course, Jews that were refugees hiding. And this was [LAUGHS] almost next door to the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they came with dogs. And [SIGHS] the fear is totally unexplainable. I remember it was a panic that we were-- we just-- where do we go from here? Out you cannot go. Where? Where in the barn? And there was a ladder in that barn leading to the attic where all the hay was. And the hay was forked into a cubicle into the barn for the animals. So we ran up this ladder with-- full with hay.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we didn't see-- I didn't see that there was a hole there where they were forking the hay into there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I fell through that hole. I hit my head into a concrete wall in that-- in that-- and I lost my consciousness. And that was good. The fear was gone. And then, after a few hours-- and my parents couldn't get me because they were up there and knew I fell down. I mean, it was horrible. They couldn't breathe. They couldn't let a sound out. And after a few hours, I found myself stretched on a bed. I was-- had a concussion. I had horrible headaches. And they couldn't call a doctor because they were afraid he would denounce us. I mean, you couldn't just call a doctor. And then we knew this is it. We cannot stay here anymore. Now, they were connected to the underground, one of the boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is one of the sons?

speaker: interviewee
text: One of the sons. And one day very shortly, I think, a few days after, a man from the underground visited us. All I remember-- that his name is Marcello. He said to us-- he was well-dressed. He was well-spoken. He wasn't a peasant. Like, he wasn't a simple man that spoke with a dialect. He spoke a beautiful Italian, a university-learned Italian. He said, look, you cannot stay here anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you the only one who was fluent in Italian?

speaker: interviewee
text: I spoke beautiful Italian because I learned to and I read a lot. My parents were-- had elementary Italian,

speaker: interviewer
text: But you could--

speaker: interviewee
text: --unfortunately.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you could communicate for them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did-- they did, too, but not so well. He said like this, we have a new tactic for you, a rescue tactic. You will be handed false ID cards. And there was in Italy at that time a whole underground economy producing those ID cards and ration cards, rescue groups. This was-- this was a ongoing thing. You will go to a village called San Zenone, which is at the bottom of this mountain. You will say that you are the priests' relatives, that you come from a town called [NON-ENGLISH], which is in Italy. You lost in the bomb raids all your documents, all your clothing-- because we looked really dilapidated already by that time, totally impoverished, like refugees as you see them in the movies. That's how we looked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have anything with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little. Very little. I knew I-- I remember I had one dress that was lengthened and lengthened with pieces and more pieces, more pieces. That's how I-- and wooden shoes, wooden clogs I had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you carry something with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe a little bag or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't--

speaker: interviewee
text: We had something.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't remember any--

speaker: interviewee
text: No suitcases. There was-- we left our suitcases in that first village in Possagno because they told us, run. How will you run with suitcases? So we had what we could carry-- knapsack, bag, and so on. We had change of underwear. I remember my mother [LAUGHS] had a bra that was in, in threads. And we had lice because we lived in a room that they-- used to be food for animals. So there was lice a little bit. There was-- that room had mice. It wasn't-- that was a part [LAUGHS] of that. But I am telling you the nice part because the nice part were the people. The poverty was horrible. The room where we stayed in this-- in that mountain was something terrible because they, they couldn't eliminate the mice because they kept that animal food in that room that they cleaned up for us. They just couldn't eliminate-- the mice was walking-- were walking all over us in the middle of the night. It was just horrible. I know, even today, when I see a mouse, I scream, like, like, horror because I'm so disgusted with mice from those days.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you eat? What did they feed you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were-- they would have fed us well but-- because they had pork. But we, we didn't eat pork so we lived on rice and beans-- no vitamins. And I had developed some-- it's called furunculosis. I had little, open wounds all over my body because there was no vitamins, no fruit, no vegetables.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents ever rethink not keeping kosher?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very angry. Actually, I never forgave them that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I rebelled later with religion only because of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you question your parents when you were there in the situation?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't question. In every other way, they were wonderful. And religion is something-- I was too young to question. I didn't question. I didn't dare to tell them that I wanted. It didn't look good to tell them that I wanted. And sometimes, the old lady wanted to give it to me behind their back. She said, take a piece of meat. They won't know about it. And I felt so guilty to cheat, that I should eat and they shouldn't. I don't want to look good now because I'm not such a wonderful person. But I think this was actually very wonderful what I [LAUGHS] did, that I didn't. [LAUGHS] Because I was so tempted. But knowing that they are not eating that-- and I will now feel good in my stomach that I didn't-- I couldn't live with that. So I didn't. I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were you hungry all the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- my stomach was not empty because I had rice. But I was very, very hungry for calories. This I remember. I needed-- my body needed something that had blood in it, you know, something that had something to bite into. And I was dreaming of good food. But that was it. And we had very little oil. They had lard. But my mother didn't allow to cook with it. So they had very little-- very little oil. So they gave us like, that much. And she would use it in one week cooking this rice and pasta and all this in one pot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Five.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about when the Germans were coming and you talking to this member of the partisan group. What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, next was we arrived to this San Zenone. Now here we are, refugees, Italian speaking. We were authentic Italians with new identification cards. Our name was Tamino. I was Elsa. My mother was Elena. My father was Arturo. He said to us the following-- your father's Italian is so poor that we are going to say that from bomb raids, he got into a traumatic shock. And he lost his speech. Your mother will have to whisper when she wants to tell you something. But you will be the spokeschild of the family. And remember, whoever comes to you, talks to you, you talk. You speak. And you lost everything in those air raids. And you are now here in this village because the priest is your [? father ?] relative. He knew about us. Of course, he was the one who cooperated. And the next person that knew about us was the nobility of the town. They-- every one of these villages had a count. And he was in the underground also. And he knew that we were Jewish. He found us a place to live, the priest. It was a very unassuming family. The man was blind. They didn't connect with anybody. So they were not gossiping about us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this right in the village?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the village. It was a very beautiful, idyllic village. It's almost like in the Fellini movies. When I saw later, after the war, I recognized it, like, authentic. It had a beautiful center of the town. And it had a City Hall that looked majestic to me. Of course, it was scary because it represented authority, which, for us, was scary. There was a school, which I didn't attend. I didn't go to school. There was a parish. There was a church. And they said, you have to go to church every Sunday.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were out on the streets, and you were living as Italians in the village?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were always at home. We were very scared to leave the house. What if somebody from Possagno comes by chance to this village and recognizes [LAUGHS] us? Here this-- who are you? Tamino, and here you were Jews from [INAUDIBLE] so on top of it, we didn't want-- we couldn't interact with people because people are curious.

speaker: interviewer
text: They want to know. Who are these people? They, they are friendly. They want to ask, why is she not going to school? Why do you never go to buy any food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we didn't have ration cards. We were not registered. We got those identification cards from the partisans, but no ration cards. Because for ration cards, you have to be registered in the municipality

speaker: interviewer
text: So were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: --in the town hall.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were fed by the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: This family had a cow. So they give us milk. And the priest came almost every three days and brought us something, bread and, and some provisions. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was-- we knew this is a very-- time of a vacuum. We knew this cannot last. We knew that [SIGHS] people are going to ask questions. And they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you--

speaker: interviewer
text: One.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were there about a month or two. He was regularly visiting us. He wanted to know if we go to church regularly, which we did. We went-- see, we had to now transfer into the Catholic religion without knowing the mechanics, without having any knowledge. He tried to convert us and baptize us. My mother was very firm. She said, look, my father was a rabbi. As you speak on that pulpit, he did. We can't.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to stop you there. And we're going to change the tape and then continue.
